So the way we Sentences are not usually isolated: they mostly fit into longer pieces of INTRODUCTION Section 25  Information Structure grammar •  267  information structure: normal order and variations The floor has had ketchup dropped all over it.

Charlotte had her roof blown off by the gale.

a structure with have + object + past participle (►   109.3).

If we  want to make something else the subject, we  can often do this by using Ketchup has been dropped all over the floor.

Charlotte's roof was blown off by the gale.

a passive verb form (►   57).

If we want to make the patient the subject, we  can usually do this by choosing Somebody's dropped ketchup all over the floor.

The gale blew Charlotte's roof off.

verb form (►   2).

make the agent the subject, we  can usually do this by choosing an active and a ‘ patient’  (the person or thing that something is done to). If we want to In many situations, there is an ‘ agent’  (the person or thing who  does something) 3 choosing       the   right    structure The children followed him  through the silent streets.

- He led the children through the silent streets.

7,000 people work for the furniture factory.

- The furniture factory employs 7,000 people.

We   can often get the most suitable subject by choosing the right verb. Compare: 2 choosing       the   right    verb For ‘ known’  and ’ new' information with as, since and because, ►  377.

There's a cat on the roof. (More natural than A cat's on the roof.) can often use the there is structure. For details, ►    20.

To  avoid immediately beginning a clause with a completely new element, we (More natural than ... My coat's on your shirt.) 'I can't find my clothes.' 'Well, your shirt's under my coat.' by our dog this morning.) Our  dog bit somebody this morning. (More natural than Somebody was bitten father last week.) My father was bitten by a dog last week. (More natural than A dog bit my (More natural than ... A very nice girl's just got married to him.) ’ How's Joe these days? 'Oh, fine. He's just got married to a very nice girl.' information generally comes at the end of a clause or sentence.
